subject: Cloud Computing
title: Data Replication and Consistency
description: A quick, practical guide to Data Replication and Consistency.

hook.text: You write data in New York. A user in London reads it one second later. Do they see the update? Maybe not.

buildup.text: Data replication copies data across locations for resilience and speed. But copies take time to sync.

discovery.text: Strong consistency guarantees all reads see the latest write. Eventual consistency allows brief delays.

twist.text: The CAP theorem says you can't have consistency, availability, and partition tolerance all at once. Pick two.

climax.text: Most cloud systems choose availability and eventual consistency. For banking, strong consistency is non-negotiable.

punchline.text: Replicate for resilience. But know the consistency trade-offs.

quiz.question: What does the CAP theorem state?
options: A distributed system cannot guarantee consistency, availability, and partition tolerance simultaneously
Cloud systems must always prioritize consistency over availability
Data can only be replicated to a maximum of three regions
